---
title: "Reflection"
page-layout: article
---

## Looking Back on BAA1028 – Workflow and Data Management Module 

This module asked me to build something I had never built before: a fully
deployed personal website, written in code, managed with version control, and
published for anyone to find. Coming in, I had no experience with Quarto,
SCSS, or Git. Leaving, I have a live portfolio that I am genuinely proud of.

That shift did not happen all at once. It happened in small moments of things
clicking. Understanding why a render was failing, figuring out why a style
was not applying, realising that a commit message actually matters when you
are debugging three days later.

---

## What I Found Hard

::: {.callout-note}
## The Hardest Part
The most challenging aspect was not any single language or tool. It was
**context switching between all of them at once**. A single page on this site
involves Quarto Markdown for structure, Python for active code chunks, SCSS
for styling, YAML for configuration, and Git for version control. Keeping
track of which language I was writing in, what syntax it expected, and
remembering to render, preview, and commit at each stage took conscious effort
throughout the entire project.
:::

Early on I made the classic mistake of making too many changes at once before
rendering, which made errors much harder to trace. Learning to work in smaller
increments – change one thing, render, check, commit – was probably the most
practically useful habit I developed.

---

## What Surprised Me

What surprised me most was how accessible the whole process turned out to be
once the fundamentals were in place. Before starting, building and deploying
a website from scratch felt like something that required a computer science
background. In practice, once I understood the structure, how `_quarto.yml`
wires everything together, how SCSS variables cascade through the design, how
Git tracks every change, the rest followed naturally.

I also did not expect to find it genuinely enjoyable. There is something
satisfying about writing a few lines of Python and watching an interactive
Plotly chart appear on a page that anyone in the world can visit.

---

## What I Would Do Differently

::: {.callout-tip}
## Lesson Learned
**Design before you build.** If I were starting over, I would spend time at
the beginning sketching out the structure and visual design I wanted: which
pages, what content on each, what the colour palette and typography would be.
Do this **before** writing a single line of code. I spent more time than necessary
restructuring pages and adjusting layouts mid-build because I had not fully
thought through the design upfront.
:::

A clear plan at the start would have made the build faster, the Git history
cleaner, and the final result more cohesive from the beginning.

---

## Skills Developed

::: {.callout-tip icon=false}
### Technical Skills Checklist

- [x] Quarto – building and deploying a multi-page website
- [x] Git and GitHub – version control, incremental commits, GitHub Pages deployment
- [x] Python – data analysis, machine learning, interactive Plotly visualisations
- [x] SCSS – custom styling with Sass variables, Google Fonts, responsive layout
- [x] Markdown – rich content with callouts, tables, footnotes, Mermaid diagrams
- [x] HTML – raw HTML blocks, contact forms, custom divs
:::

---

## Looking Forward

I am not fully certain where I will be a year from now, but I am certain
these skills will be useful wherever I end up. Data literacy and the ability
to build things digitally are no longer niche technical competencies. They
are increasingly expected across business, operations, marketing, and
strategy roles.

Beyond the professional applications, what I find most interesting is the
personal utility. The same skills that built this portfolio can build a
website for a side business, a hobby blog, or a tool for tracking something
I care about. That flexibility, the ability to build things for yourself
without depending on someone else to do it, feels genuinely valuable in a
way that goes beyond any specific job title or industry.

This portfolio is the most concrete evidence I have of that. It is not a
submitted assignment sitting in a folder somewhere. It is a live website
that I built, that I own, and that I can keep developing long after this
module ends.

---

## A Final Note on the Process

The projects on this site – the dissertation on workplace social media
monitoring, the machine learning paper on SPY price prediction, the FreshTRACK
venture – were not built for this portfolio. They were built because the
modules demanded them. What this module gave me was the ability to present
them properly: to take real work, write active code around it, and publish it
in a way that a future employer, collaborator, or client could actually engage
with.

That translation from academic work to professional presence is what I
will take most from this module.
